text: Dr. Bird and Derek argue that the Gospel of John was explicitly written for Greek audiences using Greek cosmological concepts (the Logos), not for Jewish audiences, supporting the book's critique that scholarly models focused on Jewish apocalyptic frameworks have overlooked the Greek institutional context. They note John 1:12-13 rejects genealogical claims that would resonate with Jewish readers, instead addressing a non-Jewish, Hellenized audience.
quote: those first five ten verses of the Gospel of John take an ancient Greek cosmology and apply it to Jesus that's where we get that whole concept of the word was in the beginning was the word and the Word was God that is really powerful language for the church but it didn't come from Paul it didn't come from you know it came from the Greeks it didn't even come from the Jews

text: McClellan discusses Mark 1:1-3 as appropriating imperial political language from the Priene inscription praising Augustus. Mark frames Jesus using the same "good news" (evangelios) language used for the emperor's coming into the world, directly supporting the argument that "Son of God" and related titles should be understood as Hellenistic royal terminology rather than purely theological language.
quote: this verse is actually riffing off of an inscription that is praising Augustus called the Prine inscription where it Praises it talks about the good news the evangelios uh of the coming into the world of the god Augustus uh and so mark one is kind of ripping that off and kind of appropriating for Jesus this kind of political praise that is aimed at uh the emperor

text: Connor identifies extensive parallels between Greco-Roman ghost stories and Gospel resurrection narratives in Luke and John, providing evidence of Greco-Roman cultural influence on Gospel composition—directly supporting the book's Greco-Christian framework argument.
quote: I was comparing uh ghost stories in the Greco-Roman world to the gospels of Luke and John and I thought wow that you know there are so many similarities here it is very very hard to imagine that this is simply a coincidence

text: Barker's extensive discussion of Gospel authors as educated practitioners of Greek rhetorical tradition (specifically oppositio in imitando) supports the book's emphasis on Greco-Christian framework and rhetorical sophistication shaping early Christian texts.
quote: oppositio in imitandando is is similar in that regard. They didn't use that term rhetorically in antiquity, but they did it. So, they turned things inside out... if you were very highly educated was to take something and turn it inside out.

text: Caesarea Philippi—the site of Augustus temples where Vespasian had himself entertained—becomes the symbolic location in Mark where Peter confesses Jesus as Messiah, creating a Greco-Roman political counterclaim.
quote: vespasian hatte in der stadt in israels norden in caesarea filippi mit ihren monumentalen augustus tempel sich festlich von agrippa im jahre 67 bewirten lassen... petrus nun befindet sich mit jesus und den anderen jüngern ausgerechnet dort in cesana philippi wenn er in markus acht jesus als messias als den christus bekennt

text: Zinner argues that Josephus uses "Christ" as a personal name rather than a messianic title when addressing his Greek/pagan audience unfamiliar with Jewish concepts. He parallels this to how "Buddha" shifted from title to personal name in Buddhist traditions, explaining how titulature functioned in Hellenistic contexts.
quote: he's using it as a personal name not as a title so it's not hamashiach right rendered in greek which is a title the messiah to translate this be translated as christ as a personal name... buddha was originally a title he was buddha his name was siddhartha but with time right buddha actually becomes a personal name among his his the buddhists use this now as a personal name and this is what happened in the early christian movement

text: Litwa emphasizes that ancient Greeks and Romans had a different framework for assessing plausibility than moderns. He notes Philostratus's Life of Apollonius—a Greek work contemporary with the gospels—presents miracles comparable to Jesus's, suggesting the gospels should be read within Greco-Roman cultural assumptions about what was credible.
quote: "at least one very sophisticated greco-roman author namely philosophers is wants us to believe that these things happened" (referring to miracles similar to those in the gospels)

text: Walsh identifies Mark's "messianic secret" as a deliberate literary device borrowed from Homer and Virgil—the unrecognized hero motif (thaumata)—rather than theological content. This demonstrates how the gospels employ Greco-Roman epic conventions for constructing narrative authority.
quote: the author of Mark is doing is tapping into that same motif that you find in Homer and that you find in Virgil um of Epic where the hero is not recognized... the thing we call the messianic secret, Jesus comes in, he performs his miracle, people go, 'Who the heck is this guy?'

text: Walsh notes that Matthew deliberately augmented Mark with additional Hebraic and Stoic philosophical content, suggesting that Gospel authors were consciously deploying Greek philosophical and literary conventions.
quote: matthew reads mark and goes where's the hebrew bible in that there's not enough hebrew there's not enough stoicism in this

text: McDonald's discussion of synchresis demonstrates how Gospel authors compared Jesus to Greek figures while showing Jesus's superiority—providing evidence of comparative titulature within Greek cultural frameworks, a THIN area of the chapter.
quote: we see this in the New Testament of course with Jesus being like but Superior to Elijah or Elisha or David or Moses but you find it also in Greek mythology that Jesus is superior to the likes of Odysseus or Hector or Achilles

text: Walsh argues that middle Platonic philosophical ideas (flesh/spirit dualisms, self-mastery, divine essence) appear equally in both the Gospel of Thomas and the canonical gospels, demonstrating that Greek philosophical frameworks permeated early Christian thought regardless of canonical status or heretical designation.
quote: Some of the middle platonic ideas here include things like comparing the flesh and the spirit... the idea that you require selfmastery, you should have control of yourself and your body, uh, and that you have a divine essence to yourself. All of those things are also present in Thomas and they're shared by the canonical gospels.

text: Walsh argues that gospels (particularly Luke and John) establish institutional structures and foundational narratives echoing Homer and Virgil, with Matthew presenting an "ideal Israel." This supports the book's understanding of the Kingdom as a political institution with Hellenistic institutional models.
quote: there's kind of an ideal Israel presented in Matthew but an idea of institutional structure going forward for what we might call the the church... you would get from Homer which you get from Virgil

text: Ehrman discusses how scribes deliberately added "Son of God" to Mark 1:1, seeking to emphasize Jesus's divine status and counter gnostic interpretations that Jesus only became God's son at baptism. This textual evidence demonstrates the theological centrality of the "Son of God" title to early Christian Christology.
quote: a scribe knew that heretics and Gnostics were using this to say Jesus became the son of God at his baptism. So he starts the gospel with Jesus being the son of God before the beginning, so he's the son of God already well before the baptism

text: The Gospel of Mary demonstrates explicit Stoic and Platonic philosophical influences in its soteriology, supporting the book's thesis that early Christianity should be understood through a Greco-Christian framework rather than purely Jewish lens.
quote: the answers strike a rather stoic and platonic rather than rather than a Jewish tone...in typical stoic and platonic fashion even liberates one from suffering in this world

text: MacDonald's methodology using "social identity criticism" explicitly distinguishes Greek mythologizing layers from Jewish institutional material in the gospels, treating them as products of different social locations—methodologically supporting the Greco-Christian framework thesis.
quote: by using social identity theory we can see that what you have marked in red in luke is coming from one kind of social location and the stuff that you have left over is coming out of another social location

text: McClellan recommends Michael Bird's *Jesus Among the Gods: Early Christologology in the Greco Roman World*, noting it "covers a variety of different uh texts and a variety of different approaches to understanding Jesus's relationship to God" and makes "a case that there is um that there was a concern for the ontological relationship of Jesus to God already by the time of the composition of the gospel of John." This reinforces both the Greco-Roman context (Chapter 3) and Johannine theological development (Chapter 4).
quote: Michael Bird's book, Jesus Among the Gods: Early Christologology in the Greco Roman World... it's making um a case that there is um that there was a concern for the ontological relationship of Jesus to God already by the time of the composition of the gospel of John

text: The speaker demonstrates that Matthew had to adapt Hebrew prophecy (Isaiah 7:14) through Greek translation and terminology (specifically *parthenos* for "young woman" and reworking the grammatical subject from singular "she" to plural "they") to make it relevant to a Greco-Roman Jewish audience. This supports the book's argument about the necessity of Greco-Christian frameworks for understanding early Christian theology. The speaker further explains that fulfillment operates through Greek concepts of divine manifestation and authority rather than literal naming.
quote: in order for this to still be at all relevant to anyone in Greco-Roman period Judaism we've got to tweak it a little bit

text: McDonald's mimesis criticism demonstrates that Mark intentionally imitated Homer's Odyssey and Virgil's Aeneid to frame Jesus as superior to classical heroes, providing literary evidence that the gospels engaged with Greco-Roman cultural frameworks alongside Jewish tradition to construct Jesus's theological significance.
quote: Mark wished to do for the early church what Virgil did for the early Empire to provide a compelling narrative about a founding hero like Virgil Mark used the homeric epics among his models in addition to the Jewish Bible

text: McDonald identifies the Gospel of John's water-to-wine miracle as a deliberate Dionysian characterization of Jesus, supporting the argument that Christ's theological identity draws from Hellenistic divine frameworks rather than purely Jewish sources.
quote: Jesus's first miracle is turning water into wine he offers water to the mynads who were out in the wild...the author of The Gospel of John probably knows the Acts of the Apostles and he instead of having Paul being the dionysian character it's Jesus himself who's the dionysian character

text: Lambert emphasizes that gospel authors must have received advanced Greek education to compose their narratives, indicating they were conversant with Hellenistic literary and political conventions—supporting the book's contention that Jesus's "kingdom" and titles should be understood within a Greco-Christian institutional framework.
quote: Whoever wrote this material had to be educated educated in Greek education not just someone at home they were schooled they were schooled and they knew a thing or two because when you read suetonius lives of the Caesars... these authors are aware of a Trope and they know how to write great stories about Jesus.

text: Attridge's discussion of John's use of the divine name (ego eimi, "I am") as a Philo-like appropriation of Hellenistic philosophical theology demonstrates Jesus's identity expressed through Greek-influenced theological frameworks.
quote: when Jesus says uh to his Jewish opponents at the end of chapter 8 before Abraham was egoemi that's not colloquial let's claim to be the Divine name to bear and to be the Divine name

text: John's engagement with Dionysiac wine imagery (Cana miracle) suggests the gospel positions Jesus in implicit competition with Greco-Roman religious figures, consistent with a Greco-Christian rhetorical strategy.
quote: the notion that um the wine Miracle at Cana is somehow um connected with uh dionysiac wine Miracles uh that's not a new idea...John is picking up on some motifs...from uh contemporary uh sources

text: John's realized eschatology—spiritual judgment and divine encounter in the present rather than future apocalyptic expectation—reflects Hellenistic religious frameworks over Jewish apocalypticism.
quote: we find this judgment motif that's all focused on encountering Jesus in the Here and Now...he's not exercising that eschatological function of apocalyptic judge

text: The discussion of Middle Platonism's influence on John's Gospel supports the book's argument about Greek theological frameworks shaping early Christianity.
quote: John's gospel is definitely informed by either stoicism or just middle platonism in general... they have Triads are big in platonism where you have God being um Corporal and spiritual so it's physical there's matter there's metaphysical and then there's like an intermediary mediator between the two it's a very trinitarian minded philosophical outlook

text: The discussion of Mark 13's political context—how it may encode Titus's destruction of the temple through the "abomination of desolation"—supports the book's emphasis on Greek/Roman political institutions shaping Gospel theology.
quote: when he says the abomination of desolation mark winks at the reader and says let the reader understand...I have to admit that's a pretty powerful argument

text: MacDonald demonstrates that Gospel resurrection narratives are structured using Homeric literary parallels, supporting the Greco-Christian framework. Luke's formulation "a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see I have" exactly matches Anticlia in Odyssey Book 11, indicating deliberate Greek literary influence on Christian theological formulations.
quote: luke comes along and he knows first corinthians apparently he apparently knows the polling corpus... he says a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see i have anticlia in the in in the odyssey book 11 said odysseus can't hug her because she says ziki psyches souls in the netherworld do not have flesh and bone they are the same words

text: The touching and wound-recognition motif in resurrection appearances (Luke and John) derives from the Odyssey, where Odysseus proves his identity to his father through an identifiable wound—establishing Homeric models as the literary source for Gospel resurrection narrative structure.
quote: luke's business about touching jesus and recognizing him from his wounds which then appears also in the gospel of john comes right out of the odyssey odysseus comes home and he has to show his father that he's come home after 20 years and he says look at me i am he um here's my wound that i suffered as a boy

text: Joseph of Arimathea's role in the burial narrative mirrors Priam retrieving his son Hector's body from Achilles in the Iliad, demonstrating Homeric dramatic patterns shaping Gospel natarrative construction.
quote: the burial it takes place by joseph aviramathia who plays the role of priyam in getting the body of his son hector from achilles

text: MacDonald explains how Mark's gospel models Jesus's passion on Hector's death in the Iliad—including three women mourning from afar and a figure (Joseph of Arimathea) rescuing the corpse—but crucially inverts the ending through resurrection, positioning Jesus's death as the founding moment of a political kingdom rather than a city's fall.
quote: mark is the first person to have a full-blown narrative of the life of jesus...the author appeals to the death of hector at the end of the iliad whose death anticipates the fall of a great city...three women lead in the laments watching from afar a father named priyam has to rescue hector from achilles...jesus is mourned by three women who watch from afar a man named joseph maybe the same name of his his father rescues the corpse but the difference is that jesus rises from the dead with hope

text: MacDonald identifies Dionysian motifs in the Gospel of John (heavenly stranger, water-to-wine miracle, eat-my-flesh/drink-my-blood language) that were recognized as such in antiquity, suggesting Jesus's kingdom theology draws on Greco-Roman mystery religion frameworks.
quote: when you see the evidence of the debacy on the gospel of john generally generally that he's the the heavenly stranger who turns water into wine and tells people to eat his flesh and drink his blood these are all dionesian and they were recognized to be such in antiquity

text: Miller identifies the "Incognito King" motif from Homer's Odyssey as a framework for understanding Jesus in the gospels. Jesus is presented as "the Incognito King on his way to Jerusalem" whose messianic identity must remain hidden—a pattern directly paralleling Odysseus's return home in disguise.
quote: Jesus is the Incognito King on his way to Jerusalem and no one's supposed to know it he's telling everyone to be quiet... if you're reading Homer you know the explanation

text: Miller argues that the temple cleansing narrative mirrors Odysseus's climactic return to cleanse his father's house of suitors who were exploiting it. Both Jesus and Odysseus use the framework of patriarchal restoration: "He's coming back and saying you guys are horrible... you've turned my father's house into a house of Thieves."
quote: he's returning home and he's going to cleanse his father's house... that's the climactic scene in The Odyssey... it's kind of similar in the story arc of Mark

text: Allison notes that he has "read a couple of things that made me think that Luke might have Socrates in mind" when portraying Jesus's death—showing Jesus facing execution with fearlessness and composure, mirroring Socrates's philosophical composure at death. This exemplifies how Greco-Christian cultural and philosophical traditions shaped theological portrayals of Jesus.
quote: I have read a couple of things that made me think that Luke might have Socrates in mind...the death of Jesus The Passion of Christ where he is like almost Fearless he's facing this with no emotion like Socrates going to his death

text: Walsh provides evidence that gospel writers deployed Greco-Roman philosophical frameworks, including Stoicism in Matthew and technical philosophical terminology from Greco-Roman philosophy in Mark. This supports interpreting early Christian theological concepts through a Hellenistic philosophical framework rather than purely Jewish apocalyptic categories.
quote: they're technical philosophical often greco-roman philosophy terms you know um trying to explain moral psychology trying to explain physics" and "when jesus expresses anger the way that it's described and treated in that gospel is exactly what you find in stoic philosophy

text: Walsh argues that Mark's presentation of Jesus's hidden identity—the "Messianic secret"—is not merely theological doctrine but a Greek literary trope drawn from Homer and Virgil. The hero performs miracles (thaumata), crowds are bewildered and fail to recognize him, yet he leaves. This Greek epic convention directly structures how Mark constructs Jesus's identity and kingship through audience non-recognition and textual revelation to readers alone.
quote: the thing that I argue in this forthcoming piece is that what the author of Mark is doing is tapping into that same Motif that you find in Homer and that you find in Virgil um of Epic where the hero is not recognized so the thing we call the Messianic secret Jesus comes in he performs his Miracle people go who the heck is this guy he performs a Thelma he performs what we call a miracle and people go who is he you know what is he doing they're dumbfounded they're astounded and he leaves

text: The unrecognized hero performs thaumata (wonders/miracles), causing crowds to experience a specific emotional response: bewilderment, awe, confusion. Walsh identifies this exact language pattern in Mark's Gospel, indicating Mark deliberately employs Greek epic's recognition vocabulary to structure Jesus's presentation.
quote: whenever a hero comes into the scene and is not immediately recognized usually the crowds around the hero are described as awestruck as dumbfounded as confused bewildered they don't recognize the hero they'd ask each other who is this person...the exact language you have in Mark

text: Gospel authors were educated elites—only literacy in Greek and knowledge of Greek literary conventions could enable this textual composition. This establishes that the Greco-Christian framework required educated authors capable of sophisticated literary imitation of Homer and Virgil.
quote: by the time you get to the gospels you have these texts being written in Greek and the only people who could have done it are people who are educated

text: McDonald demonstrates through the Parable of the Wicked Tenants how Mark engages in "mimesis" of Homeric epics—specifically the Odyssey. Mark transforms an earlier Q source (metaphysis) while also imitating Homeric tipos, showing the sophisticated literary crafting of gospel authors engaging with Greek cultural frameworks rather than purely Jewish ones.
quote: Mark knows that story but he transforms it... this is a mimesis... you have both metaphysis of a prototyp and you also have mimesis of homeric tipos

text: Tripp emphasizes that John's gospel, likely composed in Asia Minor, must be understood within Greco-Roman religious context, particularly where Dionysus worship was dominant. John's water-to-wine miracle deliberately engages with Greco-Roman theological parallels and audience expectations, supporting the thesis that Jesus's titles and divine status should be interpreted through Hellenistic religious frameworks rather than exclusively Jewish ones.
quote: I don't think that you can dismiss Dionysus has a has something that John's playing off of Just because John's Jewish um he's writing the people in an area of the world if he's from Asia Minor then he's really from an area of the world where Dionysus is very popular so if he wants to tell a story about Jesus turning water into wine then he he would have to know that people are going to think of Dionysus

text: Tripp demonstrates that first-century Hellenistic quotation conventions valued theological reinterpretation and paraphrasing over verbatim reproduction—"that's you being a good teacher is playing with the language to unpack its meaning." This supports understanding John's construction of Jesus's Christological claims (titles, kingdom language, theological identity) as developed through Greco-Roman rhetorical conventions, not literal historical record-keeping.
quote: playing with the language to unpack its meaning so if you come to believe for example that uh Jesus was resurrected after three days then it's your job to unpack any sayings that he had that point in that direction and to rephrase them in a way that clarifies them so it's it's also a different ethic on on verbatim recall

text: Dennis McDonald's comparative literary analysis argues that Gospel writers (especially Mark and Matthew) consciously engaged with Greco-Roman epics (Homer's Iliad/Odyssey, the Aeneid) and positioned Jesus in dialogue with the Julio-Claudian imperial cult, demonstrating Greco-Christian literary and theological competition.
quote: Dennis McDonald of course in his book um says yes...to show Mark is aware of Homer's Iliad The Odyssey um ax is aware of the Aeneid in competition with the Julio Claudio line so the Roman Imperial cult is definitely in competition with the Jesus movement

text: MacDonald argues that Jesus's saying "Eat my flesh, drink my blood" derives from Greco-Roman Dionysian mystery religion (with its dying-and-rising god mythology and ritual consumption of raw flesh), not Jewish tradition. This supports the book's thesis that Jesus's core theology should be understood through a Greco-Christian framework rather than Judeo-Christian lens.
quote: the Greco-Roman background that most people identify with this phenomenon... is it's a part of dionysian religion because Dionysus was a dying and Rising God and as a part of the ritual there was the ritual eating of raw flesh called the homophobia omafagia... the idea of eating Jesus's blood flesh and drinking his blood probably is a dionysian has dionysian background

text: McDonald explains that Jesus imitating Deuteronomy (climbing a mountain and giving law to 12 followers) functions as a "literary model" where Jesus is constructed as "the new moses." This demonstrates how theological titles and kingdom concepts are built through deliberate Greek literary composition rather than historical memory, showing sophisticated intertextual construction of Jesus's identity.
quote: sometimes we have a jesus imitating the text so that he's the new moses he climbs up on a mountain gives a law to 12 followers...it really is a literary model

text: McDonald identifies the gospels as "intertextual playgrounds" that draw on "greco-roman texts" alongside Jewish sources, describing a "strategic and sophisticated mythologizing project" where Jesus is portrayed as "superior to the likes of...odysseus or achilles." This directly supports reading Jesus through a Greco-Christian literary framework.
quote: the gospel authors are heavily indebted to homeric epics...we have a strategic and a sophisticated mythologizing project going on in the gospels...to make jesus superior to the likes of...odysseus or...achilles

text: Singer describes the Eucharist as "ritual cannibalism" rooted in pagan Greco-Roman mystery religion practice: "if you ate from the gods you would take on the powers of the Gods infused with those powers." This supports the book's argument about Greek religious institutional forms influencing early Christian theology.
quote: if you ate from the gods you would take on the powers of the Gods infused with those powers so when does that occur...it's ritual cannibalism

text: The discussion of miraculous water transformation as a sign of divine presence in Greco-Roman epiphany traditions supports the book's argument that Jesus's titles and miraculous acts should be understood through Hellenistic religious grammar rather than purely Jewish apocalyptic categories. The transformation of empty vessels (stone/bronze) filled with water into wine demonstrates divine power in a way recognizable to Greco-Roman audiences.
quote: If the transformation of empty air into wine or water into wine happens this is an indicator for those who know the stories that the god it's a god and he has power... John's gospel seems to be very much in line with like this eucharistic idea of the God's presence is in the wine and in the bread

text: Walsh's analysis of "thalma" (Greek: wonder, bewilderment) as a literary device used to signal the hero's special status through misrecognition, which Mark employs throughout the gospel to indicate Jesus's unique nature without explicit theological declaration.
quote: the word means Wonder or bewilderment and so when Jesus would go and perform a miracle and there would be a crowd around him the crowd would be struck with thalma they'd be struck with Wonder because he was doing something so remarkable so unusual

text: Walsh argues that William Wrede's influential interpretation of the "Messianic Secret" as a theological concept fundamentally misreads Mark's literary strategy—it is actually a Greek literary convention (misrecognition motif) inherited from Homer and Virgil, not evidence of Jesus's hidden theological self-awareness.
quote: when I was looking at it in Greek...the Messianic Secret doesn't capture what's going on in the text what does is to notice that this thalma language is the thing that would have...been what the gospel writers...understood as a way to indicate this guy has a special status

text: Walsh identifies the "epic tradition" as a self-conscious technique in the gospels for "establishing a hallowed figure" and establishing someone as "this person is a God," which relates to the book's argument that "Son of God" operates as a Hellenistic royal-theological title systematically applied through literary construction.
quote: the Epic tradition where you're really trying to establish a foundational text for a new movement those kinds of things I think are pretty transparent in the gospels

text: The Gospel of Thomas preserves a tradition where the disciples' understanding of Jesus's identity progresses beyond Jewish messianic and wisdom categories. While Peter identifies Jesus as "Messiah" and Matthew as "wise teacher/rabbi" (both Jewish frameworks), Thomas receives a private revelation that is too elevated to state publicly—suggesting early tradition recognized an understanding of Jesus that transcended conventional Jewish categories, consistent with the book's argument for Greco-Christian theological frameworks.
quote: Thomas says master my mouth cannot even say who you are and then jesus takes him off and speaks to him privately... I can't if I told you what he said to me you would pick up stones and throw them at me

text: McDonald demonstrates that Mark systematically imitated Greek literary models (*Odyssey*, *Iliad*, Sophoclean plays) to construct the Gospel narrative, including the Judas betrayal plot. This supports the thesis that Gospel theology is a Greco-Christian construction. He notes Luke drew directly from Iliad 7 for the Matthias lottery scene.
quote: Mark was imitating not just the Odyssey but also The Iliad and also some sophoclean plays... at point after point after point it became clear that Mark was imitating... people were taught how to write by imitating recognized models

text: Cooney discusses the bread and wine identification with Osiris and Jesus as harvest deities—both must die and be reborn cyclically. This supports the political-theological understanding of resurrection as legitimacy claim tied to natural cycles and institutional renewal.
quote: Jesus says when he tells his followers you know eat this bread I am this bread and Osiris is the wheat... both of them are Harvest gods they are dionysian Harvest Gods who must die and be reborn and not every Divinity dies and is reborn so it is part of that rebirth cycle

text: MacDonald's critique that mimesis—"the most important literary part of ancient education"—is absent from New Testament scholarship highlights that classical Greek rhetorical and literary frameworks have been systematically overlooked in interpreting how early Christians articulated theology and community.
quote: why is it that the most important literary part of ancient education is mimesis and I can't find anything about mimesis when I read new testament scholarship

text: Jesus is narratively constructed as a morally superior hero compared to Greek deities, suggesting deliberate theological reinterpretation within Greco-Roman heroic frameworks—his compassion surpassing Odysseus and Achilles, his God surpassing Zeus and Apollo.
quote: Jesus is also more compassionate than Odysseus or Achilles his God is more compassionate than Zeus or Apollo

text: The resurrection narratives employ specific Greco-Roman epic patterns, particularly Odysseus's recognition scenes: Jesus reveals himself to disciples by showing wounds on his hands and feet, mirroring Odysseus revealing his thigh wound as proof of identity—a parallel recognized by medieval Christian artists.
quote: when the Risen Jesus appears to people going to the road to Emmaus he is playing the role of Odysseus revealing himself to his father Laertes... when he's with the disciples what does he do he exposes the wounds on his hands and his feet what does Odysseus do to demonstrate to his father that he's returned he shows the wound on his thigh

text: Walsh argues that Matthew's presentation of Jesus employs Stoic philosophical frameworks rather than purely Jewish theology. Matthew removes Mark's exorcisms and "pneumatic spooky stuff" and instead emphasizes anger in ways paralleling Stoic philosophy, suggesting Matthew synthesized Greek philosophy with Jewish scriptural tradition.
quote: the way that he talks about anger is exactly how the stoics do it...a jewish writer possibly or at least somebody who's well versed in that scripture or in that world who is also well versed in the popular philosophy of his day and merging the two

text: MacDonald demonstrates that the earliest stratum of the Gospel of John portrays Jesus as a Hellenistic "donor deity" paralleling Dionysus—providing wine, living water, and eternal life. This Greco-Christian theological framework of Jesus as a divine benefactor directly supports the book's argument that understanding Jesus through Hellenistic royal/divine categories is essential, rather than purely Jewish apocalyptic models.
quote: the dionysian gospel i've called because with that title because it so extensively imitates euripides bacchae...the social identity markers have almost exclusively to do with the identity of jesus as a new dionysus as a donor deity who gives eternal life living water wine at a wedding and so on

text: The detailed parallels between Jesus's trial before Pilate and Pentheus's interrogation of Dionysus in Euripides' Bacchae provide specific evidence of Greek literary influence on John's composition. Both scenes feature an unacknowledged deity on trial before a ruling figure; both use evasive language; both culminate in the deity's apparent defeat but actual triumph. The crown imagery (ivy vs. thorns) and purple cloak directly parallel Hellenistic divine iconography.
quote: panthes so first tell me who are your people pilate your own people and the chief priest delivered you to me...dionysus i am from here lydia is my country but his true home is mount olympus jesus my kingdom is not from here his true home is with the father

text: The purple cloak imagery connecting Jesus to Dionysian royal iconography supports Hellenistic royal theology. MacDonald notes that Dionysus is consistently depicted "wearing a purple cloak called a hemation" in homeric hymns and visual tradition, and John's parallel imagery suggests deliberate use of Hellenistic divine-royal symbols.
quote: the homeric hymn to aphrodite to a dionysus um i will speak of dionysus the radiant son of semoli...around his powerful shoulders he wore a purple cloak

text: Reference to Dennis McDonald's "Dionysian Gospel" demonstrating John's gospel positioning Jesus as superior to the Greek deity Dionysus, exemplifying how gospel authors engaged with and reinterpreted Greco-Roman religious frameworks to establish Jesus's theological supremacy.
quote: dennis mcdonald points this out when he wrote his book the dionyson gospel where he sees euripides the bakkai dionysus the deity being jesus is better greater so the author john is making him a better deity than this pagan one

text: MacDonald identifies purple hemation and crown imagery in John's Gospel as mimicking Dionysus imagery from Greek art, demonstrating how gospel authors embedded Jesus within Hellenistic royal and mythic iconography.
quote: in the gospel of john pilate makes a point of dressing jesus in a purple hemation and having a organic wreath... in greek art dionysus is depicted with the crown of ivory in a purple hematia... the reader knows... he's depicted like the god dionysus

text: Lambert emphasizes Matthew's literary construction of Jesus as a new Moses figure, arguing this reflects intentional authorial crafting rather than historical memory—consistent with understanding Jesus through Greek literary convention and royal-theological archetypes.
quote: Matthew's rewriting a Moses story having Jesus the central figure... they are purposely crafting Jesus to look like Moses... that is literary that is instructive that is that is the fanciful pin of the author

text: Mark's passion narrative explicitly parallels Homer's Iliad death of Hector—including the distinctive detail of the abandoned hero (Jesus calling "Elijah" but receiving no answer; Hector calling "Deophobis" but finding only void). This structural parallelism suggests Gospel theology was articulated through Greek epic frameworks, supporting claims about Hellenistic royal and theological language.
quote: hector called for deophobis for help but he had vanished the bystanders thought jesus called to elijah for help but he never came hector then knew that he would be killed for his gods had abandoned him jesus knew that he would be killed and complained that god had abandoned him

text: The parallel between the ripping of the temple curtain (Mark 15:38) and the fall of Troy after Hector's death suggests the Gospel author structured theological claims about God's judgment and the temple through Homeric political-military symbolism.
quote: trojans mourned him as though their city had been destroyed from top to bottom at jesus death the curtain of the temple was rent from top to bottom

text: Luke 24:13-53 (Emmaus Road) consciously structures post-resurrection narrative using theophany and delayed recognition patterns from Homer's Odyssey—demonstrating how gospel authors explicitly embedded Jesus within Greco-Roman literary and institutional frameworks rather than purely Jewish categories.
quote: the Lucan author Maps Jesus onto additional cultural cultural nodes of larger Greco Roman culture... Luke's narrative has Incorporated with evidence skill a number of stylistic and dramatic devices characteristic of GRE Roman epic in general

text: McDonald argues extensively for Greek literary imitation (mimesis) of Homer and other classical texts in Gospel composition, supporting the broader Greco-Christian framework thesis. He notes that Greek-speaking authors universally learned Homeric epics and would naturally employ them as cultural templates.
quote: everyone who learned to read greek in antiquity as far as we know had been exposed to the homeric epics because it was the primary textbook... because these are the foundational texts of elite greek culture including into the roman empire

text: Singer identifies Eucharist/ritual cannibalism as a pervasive Greco-Roman religious practice that infiltrated Christianity, contrasting it sharply with Jewish sacrifice traditions. This supports the book's thesis that core Christian ritual derives from Hellenistic rather than Hebraic sources.
quote: that's the ritual cannibalism of that is was pervasive in the ancient world then you can then attain the powers that Jesus had

text: McDonald shows that mimēsis allows Mark to establish Jesus as an alternative social identity surpassing Greek heroes: "when you're imitating Homer, you're trying to establish an alternative social identity that makes your hero better or more compassionate or stronger or more pious or whatever." This supports understanding Jesus's theological and royal identity through Greco-Christian heroic and institutional frameworks.
quote: when you're imitating Homer, you're trying to establish an alternative social identity that makes your hero better or more compassionate or stronger or more pious

text: MacDonald identifies mimesis as a Greek literary technique comparing characters (syncrisis) where "Jesus is superior to Moses" or "Jesus is superior to Hector—they both die heroically but Jesus rises from the dead." This demonstrates the New Testament actively uses Greek comparative frameworks to establish Jesus's theological status.
quote: mimesis is interested in taking a recognized text and changing it for certain reasons in order to do what I would call syncrisis what the Greeks call syncrisis a comparison of characters so that Jesus is superior to Moses if it's a memesis of Deuteronomy let's say or Jesus is superior to Hector they both die heroically but Jesus rises from the dead

text: The discussion of transubstantiation traces its roots to Dionysian mystery practices predating Christianity by centuries, and explicitly notes the practice contradicts Jewish law, establishing external Hellenistic influence.
quote: the blood and wine dionysus had this in the mysteries... they practiced chewing the flesh of the meat and drinking the wine which was the blood... this goes back third to fifth century bc... it is not coincidence it is purposeful that jesus has something like this in the new testament especially when it's literally contrary to the jewish law that you can eat and drink blood

text: MacDonald identifies that Luke's version of Jesus's trial before Pilate incorporates Socratic trial elements—accusations of introducing a new god and causing disorder—demonstrating the integration of Greek institutional and legal frameworks into the gospel narrative's presentation of Jesus's political standing.
quote: his trial before pilate is different from the synoptics and resembles the trial of socrates who likewise is inc is accused of introducing a new god and disorder and you know messing up the laws of athens those are the charges that luke adds to jesus's trial in the gospel

text: MacDonald discusses how the Gospel of John employs Dionysian mystery religion imagery—Jesus as "the heavenly stranger who turns water into wine and tells people to eat his flesh and drink his blood"—showing how Jesus was mythologized using Hellenistic religious frameworks rather than purely Jewish categories.
quote: he's the the heavenly stranger who turns water into wine and tells people to eat his flesh and drink his blood these are all dionesian and they will recognize to be such in antiquity

text: MacDonald notes that Jesus becomes a Dionysian character in the earliest Gospel of John, with Acts containing parallels to Euripides (likely the Bacchae, given the context of Dionysian imagery). This directly evidences the Greco-Christian framework for understanding Jesus within Hellenistic religious categories rather than purely Jewish frameworks.
quote: acts has unmistakable parallels between the pauline section and the buckeye of euripides and jesus becomes a dionysian character in the earliest gospel of the gospel of john

text: McDonald demonstrates how Gospel authors constructed Jesus's titles and theological status through syncrisis—comparative rhetoric positioning Jesus as exceeding classical Greek heroes. This directly supports the chapter's argument about "Son of God" and "Christ" as Hellenistic royal titles, showing the specific mechanism of comparative titulature across Greco-Roman cultural models.
quote: syncrisis is a comparison of one episode or especially one character with another to show that the character in the story that's now being written is superior to the hero in another story... a Christian author saying that Jesus has the heroism of an Achilles... has the wisdom of Anor... has a compassion that is superior to that of Odysseus

text: Gospel authors deliberately employed mimesis (cultural imitation) of Homer and other Greco-Roman literature to construct theological meaning—transforming and "advertising" their position through competition with classical narratives. This represents a specific literary-theological methodology for constructing the Greco-Christian framework the book argues for.
quote: when you transform a model you're not plagiarizing it you're advertising that your position is different... Mark is trying to say that the kingdom of Jesus as the line of David is going to be Eternal when anas wasn't... this is not history this is competitive mythology

text: Mimesis in antiquity functioned as a sociological mechanism for cultural identity construction and differentiation—a systematic way of claiming cultural superiority. This supports the thesis that Gospel theology deliberately engaged with Greco-Roman institutional and heroic frameworks to position Jesus's kingdom as politically and spiritually superior.
quote: mimesis is not simply an uh an artistic project it's a sociological project it's a way of talking about how your culture is different from another culture

text: Robin Walsh demonstrates that Gospel authors engaged extensively with Greek philosophical traditions. Mark in particular employs middle Platonic philosophy, using the technical Greek terms pneuma (spirit) and dunamis (power) to explain Jesus's miraculous authority—a framework that reflects Greek philosophical categories rather than purely Jewish theological concepts.
quote: Mark is a good middle platonist who understands how pneuma Works...how that's functioning...he keeps talking about the pneuma and dunamis which is the word in Greek for power he keeps talking about how that's functioning with Jesus being able to perform the Wonders that he performs

text: Robin Walsh argues that gospel authorship should be understood within Greco-Roman literary traditions—specifically mentioning Luke's engagement with the Greek novel genre and Mark's potential knowledge of Virgil and the Aeneid. This situates Gospel composition within Hellenistic literary conventions rather than oral tradition alone.
quote: Luke is The Hangover...it's a buddy film...shipwrecks and like crazy things happening like it's like a little mini Odyssey...he's writing at a time when the Greek novel is really popular

text: McDonald demonstrates that Acts employs synchresis (comparative rhetoric showing Christian characters as superior to their Greek and Roman predecessors), supporting the book's argument about kingdom theology as political contestation within the Greco-Roman world. The comparison of Stephen to Achilles and Paul's parallels to Homeric heroes reveals a deliberate strategy to position Christian figures as morally and spiritually superior competitors to classical canonical heroes.
quote: Stephen is the Christian Achilles and you can see how the author has used the Achilles image in order to make Stephen the great Christian hero martyr... your character is better than yours well that's what's happening in the Acts of the Apostles Peter and Paul and Stephen are better than the antecedents in in the Indian or in the Odyssey

text: Mark's opening word "archē" (beginning/origin) explicitly declares the Gospel as a founding myth in the Hellenistic tradition, positioning Jesus's divine status and kingship within Greco-Roman origin-myth genre rather than historical narrative.
quote: it is a declaration that the book as a whole is an AR that's a myth of Origins and the AR refers not to the beginning of the Gospel not to the beginning of Jesus's life

text: The Gerasene demoniac narrative strategically deploys Homeric parallels to position Jesus as cosmically superior to Odysseus—transforming a classical hero-narrative into a theological claim about divine power and compassion within Hellenistic frameworks.
quote: Mark is trying to do this to show that Jesus is more compassionate than Odeus too instead of blinding him and stealing his sheep he turns the gerasene demoniac into a same man and clothed

text: Matthew deliberately removes distinctive Homeric parallels from the Gerasene demoniac story (removing the single demoniac, the sailing, the name-exchange, the departure request) to shift Mark's mythological narrative into biographical-historical form.
quote: Matthew retells the story and removes almost all of The Telltale traits that are unusual in its stories...in my view what Matthew has tried to do intentionally or not is to remove the most most telling elements of the story that make it a Mythos a myth to make it into a bios

text: Carrier discusses transvaluation—how Luke's Emmaus narrative copies the Romulus resurrection story but inverts its message: while Romulus appears in glorious visage promising worldly empire, Jesus appears humbly promising a spiritual (not worldly) kingdom. This illustrates how gospel authors constructed kingdom theology in opposition to imperial values.
quote: the ambiences narrative is it's a humble narrative jesus shows up in disguise he's not in glorious raymond and and the message is a spiritual kingdom not it's anti-worldly right so it's the message so they're borrowing the same story and they're changing these things specifically to sell to tell you that they're selling a different message

text: McDonald provides detailed analysis of the Gethsemane narrative as mimesis of Homer's Odyssey (the Odysseus-Circe scene), demonstrating that the gospel writers employed Greek literary categories to structure Jesus's passion. This supports the argument that Greek frameworks, not merely Jewish categories, shaped the core theological narratives.
quote: there's a number of affinities with that scene of the last supper and with the gethsemane episode that deserve a socratic reading it becomes even more obvious in luke but in mark...odysseus has been spending a year on the island of searcy...but they get tired of it so they want to go home...odysseus was sorrow sorrowful unto death...the visit to hades is a kind of trope a substitute for death

text: McDonald identifies the resurrection/tomb narrative as directly mimicking Homer's Iliad (Book 24: Hermes putting guards to sleep and opening doors for Priam to retrieve Hector's body). The guards at Jesus's tomb parallel Homeric conventions of divine intervention.
quote: in book 24 priyam the father of hector...hermes comes and is going to escort him...he puts the guards to sleep he throws back the bolts of the uh the bivouac...what we find in the gospel of mark is an unusual narrative about guards at the tomb who then falls are stricken like dead men when jesus comes out of the tomb that is the tomb is open the guards are put to sleep and the words for guards and for the door are exactly the same as you have in homer

text: McDonald argues that Luke extensively imitates Homer and classical Greek poetry, as does the Acts of the Apostles. This textual evidence supports the book's thesis that early Christian narratives themselves are fundamentally composed within Greco-Christian literary frameworks.
quote: if I'm right that Luke is so heavily dependent on Marcus Mark's imitations of Homer and that Luke himself imitates Greek poetry including Homer um and add the Acts of the Apostles as another place where Luke imitates uh classical Greek poetry

text: Scholar Dennis McDonald identifies direct Homeric literary parallels in Mark's Gospel, demonstrating that Gospel authors deliberately merged Greek literary narratives (Homer's Odysseus cleansing his father's house) with Jewish sources to construct their temple cleansing accounts, providing evidence that Gospel construction must be understood through Greco-Christian literary frameworks.
quote: the author of mark who was obviously familiar with both saw these two things the cleansing of his father's house and the cleansing of the jerusalem temple and had this epiphany a way to merge the two

text: MacDonald argues that the earliest recoverable layer of the Gospel of John—the "Dionysian Gospel"—portrays Jesus as a divine donor deity comparable to Dionysus, giving wine, life, and eternal pleasure. He notes that ancient Christian readers (Clement of Alexandria, medieval poets) explicitly recognized these Dionysian parallels to Jesus's theological significance.
quote: ancient readers recognize that the jesus of the fourth gospel is quite a different character than the jesus of paul or the synoptics and in fact they saw similarities between jesus and the fourth gospel and the god dionysus... jesus is from the beginning to the end a donor deity much like demeter in giving grain or dionysus in giving wine and pleasure

text: MacDonald reveals that the Q document imitates Homer's Odyssey, demonstrating Greek epic literary influence in the earliest Gospel source and directly supporting the book's thesis that Jesus tradition should be understood through a Greco-Christian framework.
quote: the author of the logo of Jesus already is imitating the Odyssey

text: The professor traces how Johannine Christianity underwent institutional divisions and schisms along Christological lines, showing "four different stages of a church with different kinds of divisions happening and therefore different kinds of Christianity developing due to these divisions," illustrating the political-institutional dimensions of early Christian sectarianism relevant to the book's framework.
quote: they're clearly written by the same school of early Christianity...You can almost see four different stages of development...Early churches also seem to split a lot...Four different stages of a church with different kinds of divisions happening and, therefore different, kinds of Christianity developing due to these divisions

text: McDonald suggests Mark may have creatively interpreted Paul's language in 1 Corinthians 11 ("the night he was betrayed/handed over") to create the Judas narrative, showing Paul's influence on Gospel construction and the Gospels' creative engagement with Pauline material.
quote: I think the author of The Gospel of Mark may have read First Corinthians... the night that he was betrayed or handed over... and then wanted to decide wanted to give a name to the betrayer

text: MacDonald's argument that "the gospels need to be seen as competitors not collaborators on the grand stage of civilization" engaging with Homeric epics and arguing with each other suggests the Greek literary-institutional context was fundamental to how early Christian communities formulated theology and identity.
quote: the gospels need to be seen as competitors not collaborators and on the grand stage of civilization and not merely as private cultic self-help literature... they're arguing with each other that's what this book that's coming out from me uh my next book synoptic Epistles in homeric epics... the clash of the Gospel authors with each other about homeric epics

text: McDonald and Miller argue that early Christian texts—like Virgil's Aeneid—should be understood as myths of cultural and political identity ("myths of Origins") shaped through engagement with dominant Greek literary traditions. This supports the thesis that early Christianity positioned itself within Hellenistic political-theological frameworks.
quote: the gospel of Mark and Luke acts are not intended to be historical they're intended to be mey of Origins... like Virgil's anid that's a Mythos of the early Roman Empire... the reader is not expected to see them as historical but as culturally engaging

text: Gospel narratives, particularly Mark's Passion account, were substantially reformulated to present Jesus as politically accommodating to Roman rule and to exonerate Roman authority, demonstrating that Gospel theology was deliberately shaped to function within the Greco-Roman political-institutional framework.
quote: mark rewrote the Jesus story to say oh no no it was a frame-up he was he posed no danger wrong Pilate exonerated him it was those Jews... mark rewrote the story to sanitize Jesus in Roman eyes

text: The discussion of how social identity markers shifted across gospel compositions—from Q's Jewish focus to Mark's Greco-Roman focus to Matthew's synthesis—suggests that understanding early Christian theology requires tracking the shift toward Greek institutional and cultural frameworks.
quote: the social identity of the q author...its concern is on making the law more compassionate...when you get to the gospel of mark...the social identity of the author becomes clearer...now the social identity markers shift from one document to the next

text: Lawson argues that Mark systematically rewrote the Jesus narrative to obscure its political-royal dimensions, denigrating the family members (James, Simon, Judah) who actually led the movement after Jesus's death, and transforming the Hebrew name "Judah" into the Greco-Roman "Judas" as a literary attack on the original political messianism.
quote: when Mark rewrote the story he changed it all so that he insulted the Jesus's family... Jesus's brothers took over James took over after Jesus and then Simon took over after James and then Judah took over after Simon... calling the person who gets Jesus killed is it's Judas it's almost like a jab... the whole rewrite the New Testament is a reversal of everything

text: Tabor demonstrates how early adoption theology (Jesus designated as Son of God at baptism) became classified as the "adoption heresy" by fourth-century church fathers—supporting the book's argument that institutional Christianity reinterpreted and rejected earlier Greco-Christian frameworks in favor of incarnational orthodoxy.
quote: it's adoption first, which is the big heresy of the fourth century, because Jesus is the one who hears 'You are my beloved son' at the baptism

text: MacDonald argues that Luke structures the Jesus narrative using Homer's Iliad model, where Jesus's death anticipates the fall of the city (Jerusalem), positioning the gospel as Hellenistic political-theological narrative. This shows early Christian theology mirrored Greek epic structures for legitimizing political claims.
quote: mark is the first person to have a full-blown narrative of the life of jesus and he's writing in the aftermath of the jewish world and he's identifying the cause of the jewish war with the inability of israel to accept the teachings of jesus

text: Tabor provides detailed evidence for identifying textual interpolations (like the trinitarian formula added to Matthew 28 by later scribes), demonstrating methodology for distinguishing earliest sources from later theological layers—supporting the book's work on identifying early vs. late textual strata.
quote: 90 of the things put in were to push this trinitarian view of uh Jesus as God so the scribes are going back through doctoring up the manuscripts
